text: You know, one of the biggest problems
I've got on this channel is time.
There's just too much stuff to review
and not enough time to do it. Plus, a
lot of things that come out week to week
might not be important or high-profile
enough for a full review. And that's not
even counting the backlog of like 500
movies, games, and TV shows that people
have been begging me to make videos
about since my channel started. Look,
I'll watch Mr. In between when I watch
it, okay? I'm only one guy. Anyway, all
of this [ __ ] led me to try out a new
series called Drinker Roundup, where
I'll pick two or three or four of the
things I've watched this week and
combine their reviews together into one
big video. If it works, it should allow
me to cover stuff that I might have
overlooked otherwise. So, let's get
started and see how it goes, shall we?
And wow, are we starting out not strong
with this first one, Return to Silent
Hill. You know, I've often said that
being a Resident Evil fan is a tough gig
when it comes to film adaptations, but
damn, it's not exactly party time for
the Silent Hill boys, either. The first
movie from 2006, Jesus Christ, that was
20 years ago now, was a pretty
underwhelming mashup of vaguely
recognizable characters and iconography
from the game series wrapped around a
story that didn't make a whole lot of
sense. And the sequel was, well, let's
just not talk about that one. But credit
to director Kristoff Gance, he was
determined to take another crack at
Silent Hill and spent years raising
money independently to get this thing
made. And now that I've actually watched
it, I can't shake the feeling that he
probably shouldn't have bothered. Just
like the game version of Silent Hill 2,
the story focuses on James Sunderland, a
troubled and depressed man who gets a
letter from a former lover telling him
to meet her at Silent Hill. The only
snag in this whole thing is that she's
been dead for months by this point, but
[ __ ] it. Let's just go with it. Anyway,
James gets himself there only to find
the town abandoned, shrouded in dense
fog, and populated by horribly deformed,
vaguely humanoid monsters shambling
around. It's basically a day trip to
Cumberald, only more welcoming. Now,
don't get me wrong, there are plenty of
locations, characters, and enemies from
Silent Hill 2. The only problem is
they've been stripped of their context
and jammed into this misfiring
psychological supernatural horror movie
that tries to be a bit of everything,
but can't really commit to a course of
action. It's not scary enough to
function as a horror movie. It's not
complex or daring enough to be a
psychological thriller, and it's not
well-acted enough to be a
character-driven drama or even a tragic
romance story. But it tries to do all of
these things, and mostly it fails. and
worse, it alters key elements from the
game in a way that breaks the entire
meaning of the narrative. The game was
the story of a man pushed to breaking
point who committed a terrible act
against a loved one and eventually
stumbled into Silent Hill as he
struggled to reconcile his guilt, grief,
and repressed trauma. Everything you saw
in the game was either a manifestation
of his tortured psyche or one of the
other poor souls trapped there. And by
the end, it was left ambiguous whether
James was actually redeemed or doomed to
relive his mistakes in a kind of
self-imposed purgatory forever. The
movie, on the other hand, [ __ ] out on
all of this by trying to make James way
more sympathetic, rewriting his
relationship with Mary to work in weird
demon cults, abusive fathers, and
supernatural rituals to explain away
everything that happened to her, and
even giving them a happy ending when
it's all said and done. It all adds up
to layers of unnecessary tacky, clichéed
horror movie backstory like this is
Shelby Oaks or something, and it robs
the film of its emotional impacts. I
definitely can't fault Ganzy's passion
for the source material, but man, I do
have to question his understanding of
it. Not a recommends from the drinker.
Next up, we're taking a stroll down
positivity avenue with the rip, which
sounds like something Tatiana might
suffer from after a particularly
enthusiastic session, but never mind
that. Let's just go with it. The RIP in
this case refers to drug money seized
during police raids that can sometimes
go missing if the cops in question
happen to have sticky fingers. Such as
the case here where a special police
unit headed up by Matt Damon and Ben
Affleck get an anonymous tip about a
cache of cartel money hidden in a house
in Miami. The squad soon breaks in and
secures the money which turns out to be
way more than they expected. Over $20
million in used notes. And therein lies
the problem, dear viewer, because, well,
that's a whole lot of money that can't
be traced, and it wouldn't be all that
difficult for people to help themselves.
The unit already has a bad reputation
after one of their own was murdered, and
accusations of crooked cops and shady
deals are flying around everywhere. With
the cartel threatening to move in and
take back what's theirs, the tension
starts to rise as paranoia sets in, and
each member of the team questions who
they can trust, what their motives
really are, how much the owner of the
house really knows, and whether any of
them are going to make it out of this
alive. For the most part, The Rip is a
slick, tense, well-paced thriller with a
strong cast, solid action scenes, and a
plot that keeps you guessing until
pretty late in the game. Affleck and
Damon are always reliable and bounce
well off each other here. and the
direction and cinematography all look
great. There's nothing particularly
revolutionary going on plot-wise, but
the rip is still miles ahead of the
usual Netflix slop. And if you're
looking to kick back with a decent
little action thriller, it'll definitely
keep you engaged for a couple of hours.
Last up on our roundup is Mercy. A Chris
Pratt sci-fi thriller that I don't
really want to compare to War of the
Worlds, but man, this film is not making
it easy for me. Want to watch a movie
where the protagonist spends 95% of the
runtime sitting in a chair watching
other characters do all the work in a
bunch of screens? Well, then this is the
film for you. It's set in a near future
Los Angeles that's descended into a
crimeridden druginfested [ __ ] hole. You
know, just like present-day Los Angeles,
leading to the creation of the Mercy
System that uses AI to preside judgment
over violent criminals and gives them 90
minutes to prove their innocence. If
they fail, it's execution time for them.
Let's not worry about the fact that most
people aren't attorneys or police
detectives, and don't have the first
clue about how to mount a legal defense,
present evidence, gather testimonies, or
search for clues, and would therefore be
completely unqualified for a task like
this, which is precisely why lawyers
exist in the first place. That doesn't
matter because our main man, Chris
Pratt, is actually a police detective.
Wow, that's lucky. Who's accused of
murdering his wife in a fit of rage and
then drinking himself into oblivion
afterwards. And because he was blackout
drunk, it's very difficult for him to
remember what actually happened that
day. So, he's discovering this as much
as we are. His case is presided over by
Rebecca Ferguson playing a cold,
emotionless robot with a human face
pasted on top. I mean, play to your
strengths, I guess. Rebecca. Now he's
got 90 minutes to get his guilt level
below the probability threshold of 90%
and find the real killer, which he does
by contacting various friends and work
colleagues, getting them to look for
evidence and track down suspects and
tapping into their personal
communications and social media. Oh
yeah, everyone's locked into a public
cloud service that the Justice
Department can tap into whenever they
want because that's totally not an
invasion of privacy or a breach of their
constitutional rights. What do you think
this is, the UK? The more his
investigation proceeds, the more his
shady personal life comes to light. And
he's forced to reconcile his own deeply
flawed personality while trying to
uncover a growing conspiracy at the
company where his wife worked with time
running out for everyone involved. Oh
yeah, and the AI itself even begins to
take his side and starts to bend the
rules to facilitate his investigation,
which I'm pretty sure there must be like
a million safeguards against, but
whatever. Let's just go with it. Mercy
is one of those films that probably
sounded a lot better in a brainstorming
session than it actually turned out in
reality. The idea of a protagonist with
a ticking clock death sentence given
limited time to solve a crime, unmask a
bigger conspiracy, and save their own
life is a decent concept for a
fast-paced sci-fi thriller that deals
with a lot of zeitgeisty issues like AI
and the dangers of outsourcing too much
of ourselves to machines. But man, it
really doesn't coales into a strong
movie. The action's tedious, cheapl
looking, and fragmented, mostly viewed
through shaky handheld cameras to save
on budget. The premise of using AI with
zero human oversight to pronounce death
sentences on people is weak at best and
[ __ ] insane at worst. The dialogue's
clunky and cliched, mostly recapping
things we already know or spelling out
stuff that's just been shown on screen.
And well, a movie where your hero spends
the majority of his screen time strapped
into a chair is always going to be a
hard cell. And I don't think Chris Pratt
has the range or the acting chops to
pull it off. He seems to have been
trying to branch out into more serious
and challenging roles in recent years.
And at best, I could say the results
have been mixed. Movies like Mercy,
Electric State, and The Tomorrow War
feel feel like they aren't doing his
career or his profile any favors. And I
can't shake the feeling that he's being
downgraded by Hollywood into low-budget
streaming slop. That's exactly what
Mercy feels like to me. A theatrical
movie that probably should have just
gone direct to streaming. A film that's
too dumb to make interesting commentary
on AI, too low budget and pedestrian to
get you excited for the action scenes,
and too safe and cliche to have much in
the way of interesting character
moments. In short, it's one that is best
left avoided. And that right there is
it. My first episode of Drinkers
Roundup. Now, I've got no idea if this
format is going to work for people, if
anyone's likely to care about it, or if
it's even worth my time. But if people
really do want to see me cover a broader
range of new films and TV shows, this is
probably the best way to make it happen.
Either way, I await your commentary on
the matter.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
